creator: uzsolt@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to Kurt Jaeger from comment #11)
I think the gui-cli split is useful (at least for me :) ).
I use fet once a year for about a month (I'm a teacher and I used to create the my school's timetable) and my workflow is:
- create the fet-file (which stores the datas: list of teacher, students, rooms, activities, etc.) using Qt-gui (it's easier than editing XML-files manually)
- upload the fet-file to a server (VPS) and the server (hopefully) will generates the timetable

The GUI on server is unnecessary but on my desktop the GUI is essential. So I would install fet-gui on desktop and fet-cli on server (and wouldn't need to install it from ports). This split is similar to editors/vim (GTK2) and editors/vim-console (without any GUI).
As the Porter's Handbook writes: "a port can have a normal version with many features and quite a few dependencies, and a light “lite” version with only basic features and minimal dependencies" and "If a port has a “lite” slave port, the slave port can be removed, and the port can be converted to flavors with...".
